primary_reason: This communication explains surveillance-design challenges and proposes integrated data and analytic innovations for early outbreak detection.
This influenza application builds and tests a weather-based rough-set and support-vector-machine early-warning model.
This analysis combines surveillance and investigation data to estimate transmission and evaluate border screening and school-holiday effects.
This article specifies an integrated WHO strategy for HIV drug-resistance indicators, surveys, and laboratory surveillance.
The multicountry application demonstrates transferable programmatic indicators for HIV resistance surveillance.
This Caribbean implementation evaluates WHO HIV drug-resistance early-warning indicators across national treatment programs.
The study applies and compares program indicators for monitoring HIV resistance risk.
This pilot applies transferable early-warning indicators to evaluate antiretroviral service delivery.
This pilot uses standardized HIV drug-resistance indicators to identify deficient clinic performance and implement corrective actions.
This study combines cohort retention analysis with HIV drug-resistance early-warning indicators to assess ART services.
This national clinic assessment applies WHO early-warning indicators to identify ART-system factors that can promote HIV drug resistance.
This paper develops polynomial-expansion methods for propagating reproduction-number uncertainty through epidemic forecasts and demonstrates them with H1N1 data.
This article identifies surveillance biases from culture-independent diagnostics and recommends validation and subtyping-compatible corrective strategies.

This paper develops network epidemic models and derives how random edge removal changes the reproduction number.

This paper formally revises notation and equations for core vector-borne transmission metrics including vectorial capacity and reproduction number.
This study evaluates rapid benchtop whole-genome sequencing for resolving healthcare-associated transmission and outbreak surveillance.

This study compares change-point methods and EARS for detecting subtle changes in syndromic influenza surveillance time series.
